data.title: PolarCool to Provide Concussion Treatment System to German Football Club
data.description:
PolarCool (POLAR.ST) will supply its PolarCap concussion treatment product under an agreement with 1. FC Köln, a German Bundesliga 2 football club.

The two-year commercial contract, which the medical technology company considers "financially limited," will be preceded by an initial evaluation period.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1047 ET - Amazon's planned used-car business would likely be more of a threat to third-party listing sites like CarGurus than online marketplace Carvana, Bank of America analysts Michael McGovern and Justin Post say in a report. Amazon Autos, which lets customers browse and buy new Hyundai vehicles, is adding used cars to its repertoire soon, General Manager Fan Jin said on a podcast this week. Amazon's expansion could help drive consumer interest in buying vehicles online. Amazon's model is closer to third-party platforms, while Carvana's broader selection beyond Hyundai models gives it a competitive edge, the analysts say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0532 ET - Nvidia shares edge higher premarket after Chief Executive Jensen Huang said in a Financial Times interview that the company would spend hundreds of billions of dollars on chips and other electronics manufactured in the U.S. over the next four years. Nvidia stock closed 1.8% higher at $117.52 Wednesday. Shares are up 1.3% Thursday premarket, touching a high of $119.55. Huang's remarks come as Nvidia hosts a weeklong AI event in San Jose, where the company unveiled a new line of chips called Rubin that are expected to be available in the second half of 2026. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0500 ET - Investors might be too optimistic about Xiaomi's EV business, Morningstar analyst Dan Baker writes in a note. The company's shares look overvalued, with the current P/E ratio at around 52X well above its historical range, Baker says. He thinks current valuations reflect an overly optimistic view of the EV business, they say. Morningstar forecasts five-year average revenue growth of 15% for Xiaomi, and projects its EV business to sell around 570,000 cars per year by 2029. Morningstar estimates the company's EV gross margin to rise to 25% by 2028. It raises its fair value estimate on the stock by 30% to HK$30.00. Shares last closed at HK$56.50.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0424 ET - Tencent's acceleration in its AI expenditure and its multi-model approach is expected drive diversified monetization opportunities, HSBC analysts say in a research note. HSBC raises Tencent's 2025 capital expenditure commitment assumption to CNY90 billion from CNY77 billion in 2024. The analysts note that the company's AI monetization should go beyond cloud to cover a range of scenarios, especially through Tencent's existing ad business and its Weixin ecosystem. HSBC raises its 2025-26 topline estimates for Tencent by 1% on higher ad and cloud estimates, with more conservative overseas game assumptions. The bank maintains a buy call on Tencent and raises its target price to HK$630.00 from HK$610.00. Shares are last at HK$519.50. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0348 ET - Advanced Info Service appears to have an optimistic outlook for revenue growth and mobile handset margins, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Wasu Mattanapotchanart, citing the CFO's comments at a recent meeting. The company's mobile revenue growth is expected to outperform Thailand's GDP growth slightly over next few years partly due to rising prepaid revenue, according to the CFO. The prepaid revenue growth should be supported by personalised packages, including unlimited data and premium subscriptions for video-content applications, the analyst notes. Also, management aims to lift handset margins further by increasing the proportion of retail sales compared with wholesale revenue. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of THB312.00 on the shares, which are 1.5% higher at THB270.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0057 ET - Tencent's AI investment strategy should propel long-term structural growth for all its business units, Daiwa analysts say in a research note. Daiwa believes Tencent is best positioned within China's internet sector to benefit from the increasing adoption of AI. Tencent's AI-powered features could drive marketing services revenue up 23% on year in 1Q, the analysts say. As the tech giant allocates more chip capacity to external clients, the analysts expect fintech and business services revenue growth to accelerate to 10% on year in 1Q. Daiwa lifts its 2025-2026 Tencent earnings per share estimate by 4%-5% to reflect a better outlook. Daiwa raises the stock's target to HK$670.00 from HK$565.00 and maintains a buy call. Shares are last at HK$526.00.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2236 ET - Tencent could further expand its user base by introducing AI solutions to various consumer-facing products, Citi analysts say in a research note. As constraints over graphic processing units gradually ease, following its stepped-up capital expenditure investment, Tencent's cloud revenue could accelerate in 2025, the analysts say. Citi raises Tencent's 2025 revenue and earnings estimates by 3.3% and 7.7%, respectively, reflecting the company's latest results. Citi maintains a buy rating on Tencent and raises its target price to HK$681.00 from HK$648.00, citing its solid execution track record, steady gaming profit, and likely rising revenue from its ad and business services, boosted its AI adoption. Shares are last down 2.4% at HK$527.00. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2211 ET - Tencent could become a dominant AI player in China given its unparalleled breadth and depth of social and user insight, Barclays analysts say in a research note. They note Tencent had a bit of a late start in the AI race, but say it has generally caught up with the rest of China's AI leaders. Tencent's around $5 billion capital expenditure in 4Q should build a foundation for strong cloud and AI growth in 2025, the analysts say. Meanwhile, Tencent's consistent around $20 billion annual shareholder return through dividends and buybacks signals that management and shareholder interests are aligned, the bank says. Barclays raises Tencent ADR target price to US$88.00 from US$66.00. Shares last closed 0.1% higher at US$69.59. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2157 ET - The merger of Axiata Group and Sinar Mas's Indonesian telecom operations should strengthen market position, but integration costs over the next one to two years may weigh on earnings, Kenanga IB analyst Kylie Chan Sze Zan says in a note. Also, the merged XL Axiata and Smartfren entity could face pressure on growth in average revenue per user due to inflation and competition from larger peers, particularly Telkomsel, she reckons. Axiata may face near-term earnings dilution as its stake in Indonesian unit XL drops to 47.9% from 66.5%, she adds. Kenanga cuts Axiata's target price to MYR1.85 from MYR2.05 while maintaining a market perform rating on the stock. Shares are unchanged at MYR1.78.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1355 ET - The EU's warning is "a strong signal to Alphabet that its approach needs to change rapidly," Emmanuel Mounier, secretary general at travel group association EU Travel Tech, says. The European Commission said on Wednesday that Google appears to be favoring its own services--like Google Shopping and hotel booking--over rivals' offerings. Mounier, whose group counts companies like Booking.com and Skyscanner among its members, says the EU is at a "turning point" with its DMA enforcement. "By ending Google Search self-preferencing, it can demonstrate that it can deliver real benefits to all digital businesses, including U.S. ones, as well as European consumers," he says. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

1345 ET - Telecommunications heavyweight América Móvil plans to pay dividends of 0.52 Mexican pesos a share, while adding MXN10 billion to its share-buyback fund for the April 2025 to April 2026 period. The proposal puts the dividend yield at 3.6% at current prices, plus about 1.1% if the buyback fund is used up, analysts at Banorte say in a note. The implications are neutral for the stock, they say. "The company's outlook remains favorable, back by sustained demand and good growth despite a challenging ecoomic environment in coming quarters." América Móvil shares are down 0.9% on the Mexican stock exchange.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1340 ET - The EU's enforcement of its new tech antitrust rulebook "has been marked by opaque, discretionary process that lacks predictability and proportionality," Daniel Friedlaender, senior vice president and head of tech lobby group CCIA Europe, says. The bloc's competition watchdog moved forward with its scrutiny of Apple and Google under the Digital Markets Act. CCIA counts both Apple and Google among its members, alongside other tech giants like Meta and Amazon, which also have to comply with the law. "The commission's apparent push to impose granular design changes on digital products, impacting the user experience and security, raises questions about whether all the tradeoffs for consumers and business users have been properly considered," he says.(edith.hancock@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1153 ET - European buyout shop CVC is taking a cautious look at 2025 when it comes to investment sales, guiding for exits "at, or slightly above" the EUR13.1 billion reached in 2024. Though exits more than doubled from 2023, they were lumpy and dropped in the second half of the year. Deployments, meanwhile, reached EUR25.6 billion in 2024, up 71% from the year earlier, and were largely driven by a PE recovery as the firm activated in May its flagship EUR26.8 billion Europe/Americas buyout fund. PE deployment reached EUR13.3 billion in 2024, compared with EUR4.2 billion in 2023. (maria.armental@wsj.com; @mjarmental)

1024 ET - Swiss retail clients are increasingly turning to European stocks trading, the CEO of online bank and trading platform Swissquote says in an interview. Its customers trade mostly in Swiss stocks and U.S. stocks--its second largest market for asset trading--but are now looking more at European companies, Marc Buerki says. Swiss customers tend to have a good level of financial knowledge and chase good performance at home and abroad. "They go a little bit where the wind is blowing, so to say, suddenly they see that the European market is quite positive, then they will not hesitate to invest in euro products," the executive says. Buerki also notes that there has been an increase in client numbers at its bank in Luxembourg.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0934 ET - A rise in household mortgage debt at the start of the year pushed up the credit liabilities of Canadian households. Statistics Canada data shows credit liabilities were up 0.3% during January to C$3.043 trillion, marking deceleration from 0.4% growth in December. Household mortgage debt rose 0.4%, for annualized growth of 4.8% in January following 4.4% growth the prior month. The central bank in January lowered its policy interest rate a sixth time in a row, taking it to 3%. It cut again last week by a further quarter point. Household non-mortgage loan debt edged down 0.1% in January, following December 0.7% rise. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0627 ET - Banco de Sabadell's investors should have all the information on the conditions the Spanish government might impose on the hostile takeover offer from BBVA before deciding how to vote, its chairman Josep Oliu says at a press conference ahead of its AGM in Barcelona. The offer, which was rebuffed by Sabadell and currently implies a discount, needs just under half of voting rights to pass. While the government can't stop the bid, it can legally block a merger, making Sabadell a BBVA subsidiary if it wins the shareholder vote. Sabadell doesn't expect its retail shareholders--which make up around half of its registry--to tender their shares, and institutional investors are expected to decide once the prospects are published, Oliu says at a press conference ahead of the bank's annual general meeting. But clarity is needed on whether imposed remedies could affect the offer's attractiveness, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0555 ET - Banco de Sabadell's mission is to support Spanish businesses and customers, not to compete with U.S. or European banks, its chairman says at a press conference ahead of its AGM in Barcelona. TSB Bank, the U.K. lender that Sabadell has owned since 2015, is a capital investment which is currently generating good returns, Josep Oliu says. The Spanish lender is trying to fend off a hostile takeover bid from larger peer BBVA, which makes most of its business from Mexico and Turkey. He expects that the offer will be put to Sabadell shareholders for a vote in July or in September. Spain's competition and financial markets regulators are yet to issue a decision after reviewing the proposed merger. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0520 ET - CVC Capital Partners' first set of full-year earnings are a mixed bag, Citi says in a research note. The group's print was strong but its near-term outlook for performance-related earnings is weaker than expected, analysts write. The private equity and investment advisory firm guided for growth this year above last year's but well below its mid-term range. The acceleration of CVC's initiatives in private wealth and insurance are interesting given that the company isn't a first-mover in either channel and it could be hard for investors to quantify incremental upside, they note. "We see upside to guidance and expect CVC to outgrow peers over time." Shares in Amsterdam rise 3.5% to 19.9 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0508 ET - Lloyds Banking Group is set to see faster and more sustainable income growth than NatWest and Barclays, HSBC says in a research note. This should allow the U.K. domestic bank to see the biggest uplift in return on tangible equity, analyst Robin Down writes. The bank's more diversified business model and fee income growth makes it the best placed to benefit as costs and risk-weighed assets inflation remains low, he notes. "Indeed that growth is enough to offset our concerns around motor finance provisions" Down says, referring to U.K.'s regulatory probe on car loans. HSBC lifts the stock rating to buy from hold and its target price to 85 pence from 61 pence. Shares edge up 0.1% to 70.66 pence and are up 29% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0502 ET - Prudential PLC's results confirm the group's value on a price-to-book basis but also show that it has more work to do to convince investors that new business value growth is compelling, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the insurance and investment company's 2024 print. New business met consensus for the year after an underwhelming end of the period as it saw a steeper-than-expected slowdown in China, analyst William Hawkins writes. He adds that a lot hangs on its guidance of more than 10% new business profit growth for 2025. Elsewhere, he notes that the dividend beat and the solvency and liquidity figures generally confirm that these aren't binding constraints to growth or the capital management outlook. Shares in London rise 1.3% to 784 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0452 ET - Commerzbank has been "pretty successful" at creating value for its stakeholders and aims to continue on this path as a standalone unit, its CEO says at a London conference. Bettina Orlopp says she hasn't had any conversations with UniCredit CEO Andrea Orcel since the ECB approved the increase of the Italian bank's stake in Commerzbank to 29.9%. She has had held investor meetings as is usual to do with the bank's important shareholders around its results and investor day, she says. Orcel has said the bank is comfortable in waiting until 2027 to see how Commerzbank delivers on its targets before assessing its options. "The whole game changes if you go beyond 30%...then the whole procedures are completely different and we are not talking about that," she says, referring to the threshold at which it is mandatory to launch a takeover offer under German regulations. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

2354 ET - ANZ Group replaces National Australia Bank as Morgan Stanley analysts' top pick among Australia's major banks. Both lenders are rated equal-weight by MS analysts, but they highlight ANZ's wider-than-usual discount to peers following its share-price underperformance through 2024. They tell clients in a note that they also prefer ANZ for its differentiated institutional bank, positive momentum in Australian mortgages, good cost discipline and its much-improved risk profile. They have some concerns about ANZ's relatively low provision coverage and limited flexibility on capital management, but raise their target price 7.7% to A$29.30. Shares are up 1.7% at A$29.22. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2352 ET - National Australia Bank's senior-management changes raise the lender's execution risk and prompt Morgan Stanley analysts to lower their rating on the stock to equal-weight from overweight. They also worry about pressure on margins from competition in business banking, lower-than-market growth rates in mortgage lending, and less chance of further capital-management initiatives. They tell clients in a note that the factors will likely weigh on near-term share-price performance. However, NAB still has a solid recent track record on costs and healthy levels of provisions, they add. MS cuts its target price 7.0% to A$34.80. Shares are up 1.2% at A$33.05. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1244 ET - BBVA's hostile takeover bid for smaller peer Sabadell looks like a "shaky" transaction due to the strong negative social reaction in Spain and potential no-merger scenario the suitor is considering, Sabadell's CEO says at a London conference. If a merger doesn't go through, BBVA is envisaging a scenario under which the two separate banks operate under one entity. This structure would make it impossible to achieve the targeted synergies, Sabadell's CEO Cesar Gonzalez Bueno says. The competition authority CNMC, which is currently undertaking a phase 2 review, probably won't take any hard stance on the deal given their methodology, which is based on solving competition issues with behavioral elements, as opposed to structural ones, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1208 ET - Oil futures shake off early lethargy and move higher as the U.S. Treasury Department steps up enforcement against Iranian crude oil, targeting a Chinese "teapot" refinery and numerous vessels transporting Iranian crude. Teapot refineries are small independent refineries in China that buy Iranian oil. Tighter sanctions against Iranian oil are seen as a potentially bullish counterweight to bearish considerations in the market such as OPEC+ plans to start unwinding output cuts, talks to end the Russia-Ukraine war that could see added Russian barrels on the market, and U.S. trade wars curbing global growth and oil demand. WTI is up 1.6% at $68.23 a barrel and Brent is up 1.5% at $71.86. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0948 ET - Oil futures trade in a tight range as the market navigates geopolitical events--bullish renewed hostilities in the Middle East, bearish talks to end the Russia-Ukraine war--and concerns supply will start outpacing demand. But "the elephant in the room is still the uncertainty created by the Trump tariffs," Ritterbusch says in a note, referring to plans for reciprocal trade tariffs. "Although most markets are looking ahead to April 2 when the rollout of widespread tariffs is expected, we feel that enforcing or implementing the tariffs could delay any actual impact on the U.S. economy for several weeks or months." Tariffs may also be tempered or delayed if stocks sell off again, the firm adds. Front month WTI is off 0.1% at $67.10 a barrel and Brent is up 2 cents at $70.80 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0446 ET - RWE's performance for 2024 was positive but JPMorgan analysts expect consensus expectations will reflect the German energy company's cut to capital expenditures, lower growth in the U.S. and higher return requirements targets. The analysts say they see the company's targets for 2027 and 2030 as credible. They add that consensus is broadly in line with the company's per share earnings targets for those two years. The analysts say that 2025 is the year of a trough in earnings for RWE as exceptional prices and volatility are now behind the company. "We expect consensus now to reflect a mix of earnings growth from capex and share buybacks." Shares trade 3.7% lower at 31.83 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0344 ET - RWE's cut to capital expenditures and targets for higher returns ought to be well received, RBC Capital Markets analyst Alexander Wheeler says in a research note. The German energy company reiterating its medium-term targets should also be seen as a positive, the analyst says. Consensus expectations for this year are in line with RWE's targeted range for adjusted net profit and adjusted Ebitda, the analyst says. The company said that it would be more strict with its investment criteria, translating to 10 billion fewer euros planned to be spent in the 2025 to 2030 period than previously planned. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

1517 ET - Oil futures rise in a choppy session marked by mixed U.S. inventory data and geopolitical news. The EIA reported a 1.7 million barrel increase in U.S. crude oil stocks, which was above the 500,000 barrel estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. Gasoline stocks fell less than expected as demand eased from the week before, while distillate inventories saw an unexpected 2.8 million barrel decline. On the geopolitical front, a Russian pledge not to attack Ukrainian energy infrastructure was tempered by the idea that a ceasefire is still some time off. The Fed kept interest rates steady as expected, but lowered its growth projections while raising its inflation outlook. WTI settles up 0.4% at $67.16 a barrel, and Brent gains 0.3% to $70.78 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1051 ET - In an outlook for 2025, Citi Research raises its price forecast for gold futures to $3,200 per troy ounce. This target is expected to be reached within the next three months, and is a $200 uptick from the firm's previous outlook. Gold may climb as high as $3,500 an ounce by the end of the year, if fears of growing U.S. economic woes take hold. "If President Trump's plan to lower energy prices fails to sufficiently offset the tariff-driven inflation, U.S. labor market deterioration accelerates, and equity market drawdown continues, elevated levels of household fear… can drive gold price to reach new records," says Citi. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0617 ET - Palm oil rose in the Asian trading session. Prices have been driven by Indonesia's plans to raise taxes on palm oil exports, a move that would raise Malaysia's palm oil price competitiveness, alongside data indicating softer production in Malaysia early March, analysts at Kenanga Futures wrote in a note. Lower seasonal supply may continue to support prices, they said. The analysts see support and resistance for the June futures contract at MYR4,280 and MYR4,440, respectively. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for June delivery rose MYR26 to MYR4,415/ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0559 ET - Lanxess's 2025 outlook is below consensus expectations and might come as a bit of a disappointment to market participants, Warburg's Oliver Schwarz says in a research note. The German chemical company reported preliminary results in January and the final results were in line. However, the company's 2025 outlook seems a little weak, with Lanxess guiding for Ebitda excluding exceptional items of 600 million to 650 million euros and a company-compiled consensus currently expecting the figure at 674.8 million euros, the analyst says. The outlook factors in expected savings of 40 million euros, but also a prebuying effect of 20 million euros in the fourth quarter, according to Warburg. Shares fall 8.8% to 28.12 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0307 ET - Comex Gold futures' uptrend is still intact, according to the daily chart, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a research report. Despite the precious metal posting a 'Doji' candlestick that indicates neutral market sentiment on Wednesday, bullish momentum remains reflected by the upward-sloping relative strength index, the analyst says. "We anticipate the positive price action to follow through for the immediate session," says Chai, who adds that the precious metal is on track to test the $3,100/oz level. On the downside, immediate support is at $2,900/oz, followed by subsequent support at $2,850/oz. Spot gold edges 0.1% lower to $3,046.20/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2249 ET - Iron ore falls in early Asian trade amid softening sentiment due to signs of weakness in China's steel industry, the ANZ Research team writes in a note. They point to prices of futures for steel rebar, which is used for construction, hitting a six-month low. This comes as data indicates continued weakness in China's housing prices and new construction starts, they add. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.9% lower at CNY758.5 a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

2207 ET - Geo Energy Resources' share price may extend gains should it sustain the recent breakout on the technical chart, Phillip Securities Research's Zane Aw says in a research report. The share price broke out of an ascending triangle pattern last week accompanied by high volume, the analyst notes. Also, the share price is holding above the 5- and 10-day simple moving averages, with the two SMAs above the 20-day SMA, the analyst says. These offer potential bullish reversal signals, suggesting the share price could head higher. Resistance levels are pegged at S$0.370 and S$0.395, the analyst adds. Shares are unchanged at S$0.315. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1447 GMT - Amazon's planned used-car business would likely be more of a threat to third-party listing sites like CarGurus than online marketplace Carvana, Bank of America analysts Michael McGovern and Justin Post say in a report. Amazon Autos, which lets customers browse and buy new Hyundai vehicles, is adding used cars to its repertoire soon, General Manager Fan Jin said on a podcast this week. Amazon's expansion could help drive consumer interest in buying vehicles online. Amazon's model is closer to third-party platforms, while Carvana's broader selection beyond Hyundai models gives it a competitive edge, the analysts say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1308 GMT - The German government could acquire a stake in Rheinmetall on national security grounds, Mwb Research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck tells Dow Jones Newswires. Germany's likely next chancellor has gained political support for a plan to raise hundreds of billions of euros in extra defense spending. But Rieck notes that Berlin still doesn't hold a stake in Rheinmetall, the country's biggest arms maker, whereas it does own part of sensors-provider Hensoldt. A state acquisition would come too late and add to previous bad investments like the one into bankrupted Swedish battery manufacturer Northvolt, Rieck says. When Russia launched its large-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, the German government could have bought all of Rheinmetall for under 10 billion euros. Now, that same amount wouldn't even secure a 25% stake, Rieck adds. Shares are down 5.5% at 1,303 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1246 GMT - Germany's "whatever it takes" stance on defense, announced by the upcoming coalition government partners, could unlock orders worth between 300 billion and 400 billion euros for Rheinmetall by 2030, dwarfing all previous estimates, HSBC analysts Dario Dickmann and Christoph Dolleschal say. Rheinmetall's current valuation still underprices the upside from Germany's funding boost, they say, despite valuation multiples already reflecting some earnings upgrades. "We see scope for significant additional upgrades in the months and years ahead," the analysts write. HSBC increases its target price for Rheinmetall to 1,600 euros a share from 1,275 euros. Shares are down 5.3% at 1,306.5 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1117 GMT - Hapag-Lloyd Ebitda guidance is in line with consensus at the mid-point of the range, UBS analyst Cristian Nedelcu writes. The 8.20 euro dividend is also higher than the 6.63 euros expected, he notes. In terms of freight rates, the bank expects the first quarter to be the strongest quarter for profitability, before sequentially declining thereafter. The higher end of the company guidance is predicated on a sequential pick-up of spot freight rates during the peak season later this year, but UBS thinks recent demand datapoints from the U.S. suggest downside risks to peak season demand for the overall industry. Shares fall 6.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - XPeng should report stronger earnings this year on likely healthy sales momentum and its new model pipeline, Morningstar senior equity analyst Vincent Sun writes in a note. The carmaker's 4Q margin improvement was stronger than expected despite pressure from promotional activities, Sun says. Management has guided for continued expansion in vehicle margin this year on strong sales, as well as for turning profitable this 4Q, Morningstar adds. XPeng's pipeline of new models should drive robust momentum in sales volumes, it adds. Morningstar raises its fair value estimate on the stock to $17.00 from $15.20. Its ADRs last closed at $23.80. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0825 GMT - Hapag-Lloyd's EBIT guidance is well below consensus so will likely lead to some near-term downward revisions, JPMorgan analysts write. The mid-point of the 2025 EBITDA guidance range of $2.5 billion-$4 billion is broadly in line with consensus at $3.2 billion, they say. However, the mid-point of the $0-$1.5 billion EBIT guidance is 17% below the $900 million expected by consensus. Outlook commentary emphasizes the fragility of the economic and geopolitical environment. Company focus in the first half of the year is on implementing the Gemini network and keeping a close eye on costs. "We remain cautious on the outlook given the rapid deterioration in container spot rates since the start of the year, despite Red Sea diversions persisting." Shares fall 4.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - XPeng will likely see further growth in shipments, market-share gains and stronger margins in 2025, Nomura analysts write in a note. Demand for its cars should rise further, driven by upcoming face-lifted and new models, they add. XPeng's vehicle margin reached double digits for the first time in 4Q, thanks to accelerated shipments and cost-reduction efforts, they say. Meanwhile, the carmaker's AI push, including advanced driver-assistance systems and robotics, should further support long-term growth, they add. A potential Indonesia factory may also pave the way for long-term overseas opportunities, Nomura says. The investment bank initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of HK$113.00. Shares are last at HK$89.05. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

1749 GMT - The surge of imports into the nation's busiest container-port complex is slowing. The ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach in February handled the equivalent of 781,995 import containers, an 18% drop from January's record high, but 5.9% above February last year. Some retailers and manufacturers have been rushing in goods to get ahead of new tariffs on China, which were imposed in February and raised in March. Port of Los Angeles Executive Director Gene Seroka forecasts a potential 10% drop in trade activity at the port in the second half of the year because of the tariffs. (paul.berger@wsj.com)

1627 GMT - U.S. agriculture is reliant on selling exports overseas, with $105 billion of agricultural exports being shipped through ocean ports in 2024, according to the American Farm Bureau Federation — constituting 20% of total agricultural production. The proposal by President Trump to institute steep fees on Chinese-built shipping vessels may 'disproportionally' punish U.S. farmers, says the AFBF in a research note. "Farmers and ranchers, already operating on tight margins, would face higher freight rates, reduced competitiveness and potential loss of market share - all while U.S. shipbuilding capacity remains years away from scaling up," says economist Daniel Munch of the AFBF. The proposal comes amid the ongoing uncertainty around tariffs, with recommendations on reciprocal tariffs expected from the Trump Administration on April 2. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: JPMorgan Stock Is the Nvidia of Banking, Wells Fargo Says. Here's Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

JPMorgan Chase stock got a strong vote of confidence from a Wells Fargo analyst, who likened it to chip behemoth Nvidia given the bank's lead in artificial intelligence and technology.

In a note on Wednesday, Mike Mayo from Wells Fargo, reiterated his Overweight rating on JPMorgan stock with a price target of $300, which implies a gain of about 26% from the stock's last close. Mayo noted he feels more comfortable with his financial forecast for JPMorgan than for "almost any other bank."

The conviction was due to the potential growth in employee productivity, increased market share, and potential margin gains, as the bank reaps the fruits of its investments in tech.

JPMorgan's tech budget for 2025 stands at $18 billion, up $1 billion from last year. It matches the banking industry's level of about 20% of total expenses and about 10% of revenue. But its absolute amount of spending exceeds all other banks, and it's spending relatively more on products, platforms, and experiences, Mayo says.

"Given market share gains, the approach is working," he added, estimating that the bank has grown more deposits at new branches this decade than the rest of the industry combined. "Tech is part of this story as AI has aided with branch locations (demographics, customer synergies, traffic, etc.)"

JPMorgan also increased its global employee base by 7,307 year-over-year as of December, primarily attributing it to front office and technology employees growth, filings show.

To be sure, the bank has made it clear that it has reached its peak modernization — meaning the pace of tech investments will slow. On a compounded annual basis, spending looks set to increase 6% this year as opposed to 15% over the past decade.

"Tech is one of the areas where we're kind of asking people to live within their means at the margin," said Chief Financial Officer Jeremy Barnum at a conference in February.

But it also means it's time for the returns to show up more evidently, lest investors get frustrated about all the billions thrown at AI. The stock is up under a percent this year.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com.

data.title: Jabil Stock Climbs After Beats Q2 Estimates, Lifts Full-Year Guidance
data.description:
Jabil (JBL, Financial) shares climbed about 5% on Thursday morning after its Q2 fiscal 2025 results easily beat consensus estimates. The company reported revenue of $6.73 billion, while EPS came in at $1.94.

The contract manufacturer also raised its full?year outlook, now expecting revenue of $27.9 billion, up from the previous guidance of $27.3 billion. It also forecast fiscal 2025 earnings per share of $8.95, surpassing both the earlier guidance of $8.75 and the consensus estimate of $8.74.

For the third quarter, Jabil projected revenue between $6.7 billion and $7.3 billion, with EPS expected to range from $2.08 to $2.48 and a midpoint of $2.28, well above estimates.

CEO Mike Dastoor credited the strong performance in capital equipment, cloud, data center infrastructure, and digital commerce, and highlighted the company's significant U.S. manufacturing footprint, as key growth drivers.

Jabil has a global footprint in 30 countries, with a strong concentration of manufacturing and engineering facilities in the United States.

data.title: TSX up Just 5 Points at Midday, Most Sectors Lower
data.description:
The Toronto Stock Exchange which was up 70 points earlier, has given up most of its gains and is now just five points higher, with most sectors in negative territory.

Oil price edged higher following a day-prior report showing U.S. oil inventories rose last month but gasoline inventories dropped, while geopolitical risk remains high on violence in the Middle East and Ukraine. Also, gold traded at yet another record high even as the dollar rose following the Federal Reserve's day-prior decision to hold interest rates steady as safe-haven buying continues to support the metal on growth worries.

But natural gas traded lower early on Thursday ahead of fresh storage data as mild spring weather continues to cut demand.

Despite the resilience that has been shown by Canadian stock pickers, the Canadian Federation of Independent Business (CFIB) said Thursday its Business Barometer long-term index crashed to an all-time low in March, dropping 24.8 index points to 25.0. The small business confidence indicator reached a lower mark than it did at any time during the 2020 pandemic, 2008 financial crisis or 9/11.

Still, Brian Belski in his latest 'Canadian Strategy Snapshot' said the bottom line for he and his team over at BMO Capital Markets is that their stance has not wavered. They believe the current Canadian equity market weakness is "all about uncertainty — period".

"As such," Belski wrote, "we continue to advise clients that their investment process requires (now more than ever) a more fundamentally driven, disciplined and non-reactive approach. Indeed, despite the increased rhetoric and negative banter that we believe have been the primary culprits for many macro forecasts being revised to the downside, the S&P/TSX fundamental outlook remains stable in our view." Belski said this is especially evident according to BMO's models regarding the recovery/improvement in Canadian earnings that began in earnest last year, "one that remains on track and the clear base case scenario".

Belski added: "As we have previously stated, fear is a powerful emotion and uncertainty is the ice that is freezing investors. However, we believe this is all about duration, and as rhetoric fades, so too will fear and uncertainty. Ultimately, we believe the stagnant revision trends that have paralyzed earnings over the last few months will ultimately shift sharply positive, helping propel Canadian equities back to new all-time highs by year-end. In the meantime, we believe investors can diffuse market volatility by utilizing dividend growth strategies while also layering in strong Canadian companies that have been unfairly punished."

data.title: Munich Re Targets US Expansion With Deal to Take Over Next Insurance
data.description:
Munich Re entered a definitive agreement to acquire Next Insurance's entire share capital for a valuation of $2.6 billion.

Next Insurance will become part of the German reinsurance company's primary insurance business, Ergo, which currently holds 29% of Next's outstanding shares. The transaction aims to support Ergo's expansion into the small- and medium-sized business segment in the US, according to a Thursday release.

Closing of the acquisition is expected in the third quarter, subject to customary and regulatory conditions.

data.title: China's 'Hermes of gold' shines despite lacklustre demand for high-end goods
data.description:
Vivian Wang bought a gold medallion decorated with a carved dragon from Laopu Gold nearly a decade ago, but forgot about the brand until last year when it appeared all over her social media feeds.

Swiping through post after post about Laopu Gold, the sales and marketing professional from Shanghai came across her dragon medallion, which was now selling for double what she paid at 220,000 yuan ($30,440.14).

Wang decided to brave the lines outside Laopu's Shanghai store in tourist hub Yu Garden to make another purchase, this time an intricate jewellery box constructed from thinly woven gold strands with a dragon and phoenix on the lid. Wang said she would like to pass it on to her 8-year-old daughter one day.

"I'm a jewellery lover, Tiffany, Cartier or Bulgari, these are all brands I have bought," Wang said, citing rival retail chains owned by LVMH and Richemont . "I have diamonds and gemstone jewellery but ... long-term it's gold that offers the best way to preserve value."

"After the pandemic, people want to pursue something more elevated that offers some spiritual sustenance ... this traditional dragon and phoenix box has an auspicious meaning; it also offers this kind of spiritual sustenance."

Laopu Gold is a notable outlier in China's luxury goods market, which has been hammered by an extended consumer malaise due to concerns over job and wage security and a prolonged property slump.

The company has blended Chinese cultural elements within contemporary designs to emerge as a uniquely homegrown luxury brand. The strategy has been fortified by its use of gold, considered a safe investment by Chinese consumers during periods of instability.

Laopu Gold did not respond to interview requests from Reuters.

But a source with direct knowledge of Laopu's business said its annual sales approached 10 billion yuan in 2024, up from 3.18 billion in the previous year.

"Laopu's average store revenue is nearly 300 million yuan, compared to the average single-store revenue of most international jewellery brands in China, which generate around 100 to 200 million annually," the source said.

Investors have been paying attention. Since its Hong Kong IPO in June 2024, Laopu's share price has surged ten-fold, from HK$40.50 to HK$725 ($93.30).

A NEW LUXURY CONTENDER

The company has come a long way since founder Xu Gaoming, a former fisheries clerk, started his first gold jewellery business in 2009 and established the brand Laopu Gold - which translates to "old store" - in 2016.

With prices for most of its popular products ranging from 10,000 to 50,000 yuan, Laopu now competes more directly with Western luxury brands such as Tiffany and Cartier.

Its exclusivity is drawn in part from a strategy pulled from the playbook of its Western competitors.

As rising gold prices in 2024 pushed investors toward bullion and coins and gold jewellery sales in China fell 24.69% to 532.02 tons, Laopu twice raised prices on its designer jewellery and trinkets. It again upped prices by 5-12% last month.

Laopu has also eschewed the spot price for gold to which other domestic competitors peg their selling rates, charging a premium for design and branding.

Its best-selling gourd-shaped pendant — a symbol of good fortune in Chinese culture — contains 24.4 grams of gold but sells for over 29,000 yuan, nearly 1,200 yuan per gram.

Other brands might sell similarly weighted products for 900 yuan per gram in line with the 700 yuan per gram price of gold.

And while gold-focussed brands including Chow Tai Fook and Lao Feng Xiang each have store networks in the thousands, Laopu has chosen to keep its retail experience exclusive.

It has just 37 stores across greater China, including Hong Kong and Macau, and now plans to enter the international market with an upcoming Singapore store.

Chinese consumers have dubbed Laopu the "Hermes of Gold".

While Chinese brands have stolen market share from Nike in sportswear and L'Oreal in beauty, they have struggled to threaten global luxury players which charge a premium for storytelling, heritage and design.

High-profile entrants in this space, such as clothing and furniture maker Shang Xia (co-founded by Hermes but since sold to Ferrari-owner Exor) and the Kering-owned jewellery brand Qeelin, have found a niche audience for products that attempt to balance Chinese and Western luxury elements.

But analysts say Laopu is a contender to fully break through the luxury glass ceiling.

"It's not an imminent threat to global luxury groups because to build a luxury brand takes a long time. You need a story, you need a history, you need the craftsmanship, but it's possible," said Jonathan Yan, a principal at consultancy Roland Berger in Shanghai.

"If you have the best-in-class design, good quality, good service, it's possible."

($1 = 7.2273 Chinese yuan renminbi)

($1 = 7.7706 Hong Kong dollars)

data.title: Thursday data buffet: Luke warm, but not undercooked
data.description:
Key points:
  • US equity indexes green, Dow out front up 0.3%
  • Comm Svcs is top S&P sector gainer; consumer staples off most
  • Euro STOXX 600 index dips ~0.4%
  • Dollar jumps; crude up >1%; bitcoin off ~1%; gold dips
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield falls to ~4.23%

THURSDAY DATA BUFFET: LUKE WARM, BUT NOT UNDERCOOKED

A spate of indicators appeared to show that the U.S. economy remains on fairly solid ground, despite the note of caution (and lowered projections) from the Federal Reserve.

Last week 223,000 U.S. workers joined the queue outside the unemployment office (USJOB=ECI), marking an increase of 2,000 from the previous week and landing just a hair below consensus.

The underlying trend, as expressed by the 4-week moving average of initial claims, is essentially sideways with a slight upward bias.

Initial claims have been range-bound in the 200,000 to 250,000 area for about two years

The mass firings of Federal employees at the hands of billionaire Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) are handled in Unemployment Compensation for Federal Employees (UCFE) program, which is reported on a one-week delay.

That number pulled back a bit but remains well above the long-term historic trendline.

Continuing claims (USJOBN=ECI), also posted on one-week lag, increased by 1.8% to 1.892 million, echoing recent survey data which shows deteriorating views of the jobs market.

"The number of people who are unemployed but who have exhausted their 26-week entitlement to jobless benefits likely has continued to creep up, and surveys of consumers' confidence are consistent with a rising trend in unemployment," writes Samuel Tombs, chief U.S. economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics.

Turning to the housing market, the sales of pre-owned U.S. homes (USEHS=ECI) unexpectedly increased by 4.2% in February to 4.26 million units at a seasonally adjusted annualized rate (SAAR), according to the National Association of Realtors (NAR).

That's 7.8% higher than economists predicted.

A 5.7% jump in single-family homes drove the gains, offsetting a 9.8% drop in condos/co-ops, which account for a much smaller slice of the pie.

Despite a slight increase of homes on the market, at February's pace it would take 3.5 months to sell every home on the market, unchanged from January.

"Home buyers are slowly entering the market," writes Lawrence Yun, NAR's chief economist. "Mortgage rates have not changed much, but more inventory and choices are releasing pent-up housing demand."

Pivoting to the manufacturing sector, the expansion of factory activity in the Atlantic region has decelerated a bit this month.

The Philadelphia Fed's Business index (USPFDB=ECI) dropped 5.6 points to 12.5, not quite as steep as the 9.6 point drop analysts expected.

A fairly pronounced slowdown in new orders, and significantly dimmer six-month expectations - likely a reflection of policy uncertainty under Trump 2.0 - were two of the report's more worrisome aspects.

The acceleration of prices paid - an inflation predictor - isn't exactly welcome news either.

Still, it's a cheerier reading than the Empire State's precipitous plunge into negative territory on Monday.

"Manufacturing may be slumping in the nation as a whole, but it is still booming in Philly, according to this index," says Carl Weinberg, lead economist at High Frequency Economics. "However, the 'boom' seems to be cooling on trend."

A negative Philly Fed/Empire state reading indicates monthly contraction, while a number above zero signifies expansion.

Next, the Conference Board's (CB) Leading Economic Index (USLEAD=ECI) decreased by 0.3% last month, steeper than analysts expected and marking an accelerated decline from January.

The index is an amalgamation of ten forward looking metrics (including initial jobless claims, ISM new orders, yield spreads and S&P 500 price performance, among others).

"The US LEI fell again in February and continues to point to headwinds ahead," writes Justyna Zabinska-La Monica, CB's senior manager of Business Cycle Indicators. “Consumers’ expectations of future business conditions turned more pessimistic."

As a reminder that the stock market and the economy are two different things, the chart below shows the LEI against the S&P 500. While the two historically move in concert, they appear to have diverged toward the end of 2022, around the time the bull market began.

In more ancient news, the current account deficit (USCURA=ECI), which accounts for trade, international investment and other transfers of capital, contracted by 2.1% in the fourth quarter.

That narrowing was likely temporary, as imports reached a record high in January as businesses tried to head off expected tariffs.

(Stephen Culp)

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1208 ET - Oil futures shake off early lethargy and move higher as the U.S. Treasury Department steps up enforcement against Iranian crude oil, targeting a Chinese "teapot" refinery and numerous vessels transporting Iranian crude. Teapot refineries are small independent refineries in China that buy Iranian oil. Tighter sanctions against Iranian oil are seen as a potentially bullish counterweight to bearish considerations in the market such as OPEC+ plans to start unwinding output cuts, talks to end the Russia-Ukraine war that could see added Russian barrels on the market, and U.S. trade wars curbing global growth and oil demand. WTI is up 1.6% at $68.23 a barrel and Brent is up 1.5% at $71.86. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1107 ET - U.S. natural gas futures add to early losses as the EIA reports the first increase in natural gas inventories since the first week of November. Gas in underground storage rose by 9 billion cubic feet last week to 1,707 Bcf, reducing the deficit against the five-year average to 190 Bcf from 230 Bcf the week before. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey had predicted on average a 12 Bcf storage draw for last week. Below-average inventories have kept futures prices above $4 despite mild March weather following a colder-than-normal start to the year. The Nymex front month is off 3.9% at $4.081/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1104 ET - Citi Research says that the implementation of tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and the EU would have a 'significant impact' on the amount of inventories for corn and soybeans in farmer inventories by the end of the year. The firm currently views the most-likely outcome of the present trade situation as tariffs on China stick while the threats against other trading partners don't materialize — in which the stocks-to-use ratios of U.S. crops look to improve. But additional tariffs, along with a potential ceasefire and peace in the Russia-Ukraine war, may drag on grain futures, says the firm. "Prices on soy and corn could be more sensitive to tariff developments, while [soft red winter] wheat prices should get dragged down by lower row crop markets," says Citi. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1051 ET - In an outlook for 2025, Citi Research raises its price forecast for gold futures to $3,200 per troy ounce. This target is expected to be reached within the next three months, and is a $200 uptick from the firm's previous outlook. Gold may climb as high as $3,500 an ounce by the end of the year, if fears of growing U.S. economic woes take hold. "If President Trump's plan to lower energy prices fails to sufficiently offset the tariff-driven inflation, U.S. labor market deterioration accelerates, and equity market drawdown continues, elevated levels of household fear… can drive gold price to reach new records," says Citi. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1031 ET - Live cattle trading on the CME is up, with the contract rising for seven consecutive trading sessions if the positive trend holds for today. One factor supporting gains is a storm that hit the Midwest this week. Winter Storm Nyla brought ice and snow to growing states, giving the soil a welcome boost in hydration ahead of spring planting. The U.S. Drought Monitor says Nebraska and Texas have large patches of extreme to exceptional drought as precipitation there remains elusive. The NOAA says that states in the Plains are parched ahead of spring planting. Lean hogs are up 1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1021 ET - Recent rainfall in Brazil and Argentina is supporting better yields of crops being harvested there, analysts say. The rainfall improved the outlook for Brazil's corn and soybeans, as well as coffee, according to ADM Investor Services in a note. The increased precipitation is expected to continue through the end of the month. Last week, the Rosario Board of Trade said that better rainfall in Argentina was helping crops there as well, helping mitigate damage caused to the soybean crop during January's drought conditions. CBOT soybeans are 0.2% higher, while corn rises 1.1%. Wheat is down 1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1008 ET - An end to Western sanctions on Russia would have a limited economic impact, Capital Economics' Nicholas Farr writes in a note to investors as talks between Moscow and Washington lift hopes of a detente. Commodity prices surged following Western sanctions imposed following the 2022 escalation of the Russia-Ukraine conflict. They have since largely eased, however, and a reversal in measures wouldn't have much impact, Farr says. The Russian economy would receive a boost from investment, goods imports and potential access to the global financial system. However, rapprochement could be lukewarm even without sanctions, Farr says. "A fundamental shift in Russia's political and business environment, as well as its relationship with the West, would be needed to boost its long-run growth outlook," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0948 ET - Oil futures trade in a tight range as the market navigates geopolitical events--bullish renewed hostilities in the Middle East, bearish talks to end the Russia-Ukraine war--and concerns supply will start outpacing demand. But "the elephant in the room is still the uncertainty created by the Trump tariffs," Ritterbusch says in a note, referring to plans for reciprocal trade tariffs. "Although most markets are looking ahead to April 2 when the rollout of widespread tariffs is expected, we feel that enforcing or implementing the tariffs could delay any actual impact on the U.S. economy for several weeks or months." Tariffs may also be tempered or delayed if stocks sell off again, the firm adds. Front month WTI is off 0.1% at $67.10 a barrel and Brent is up 2 cents at $70.80 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0925 ET - CBOT corn futures are up 0.8% pre-market, with a weaker dollar in recent weeks seen as a catalyst for importers to buy more U.S. exports of corn. Today's strong export sales report for corn may be evidence of this, says Argus in a note — adding that a period of sliding corn prices along with the weaker U.S. dollar makes corn export sales total this week of keen interest. The USDA reported that corn export sales for the week ended March 13 totaled 1.56 million metric tons, which is higher than forecast by analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal. However, the ICE dollar index is up 0.5% today. Soybean futures are down 0.3% pre-market, and wheat falls 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0905 ET - U.S. natural gas futures give back some gains as the market awaits a weekly storage report from the EIA that is expected to show a small withdrawal with the high-demand winter season approaching an end. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey predict a 12 Bcf reduction in inventories for last week, including several who see a small storage build. A storage deficit of more than 600 Bcf versus year-ago levels is "a big reason why prices have been strong over the past month and holding $4," forecaster NatGasWeather.com says in a note. The Nymex front month is off 1.5% at $4.184/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0846 ET - Darden Restaurants missed Wall Street's 3Q comparable sales forecasts, but multiple analysts seemed to expect it and the market hasn't punished the stock too harshly. Comparable sales rose just 0.7% overall. Darden's Olive Garden and LongHorn Steakhouse chains both underperformed analyst estimates, but Oppenheimer analysts recently said that was a possibility given sloppy industry data for January and February due to adverse weather in parts of the country, a challenging flu season and overall consumer weakness. UBS analysts recently said investors were gearing up for a comps miss. Shares are flat premarket at $188.(dean.seal@wsj.com)

0757 ET - Gold futures hold steady after setting a new record earlier in the session. Futures are flat on $3,041.90 a troy ounce, after rising as high as $3,065.20/oz earlier in the session. The precious metal's gain reflect safe-haven demand amid economic fears--driven by escalating trade tensions--and geopolitical worries from Middle East hostilities, Tradu.com's Nikos Tzabouras says in a note. The U.S. Federal Reserve's updated economic projections now have a stagflationary tilt, with officials expecting weaker growth and greater unemployment and inflation, Tzabouras writes. Nevertheless, a downward correction in gold prices would be reasonable as Fed Chair Jerome Powell hinted at transitory inflation effects from tariffs and market sentiment improved after the Fed's decision to hold interest rates steady. U.S. President Trump's communications with Russian and Ukrainian counterparts have also helped cool geopolitical tensions, Tzabouras adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Orla Mining Kept at Outperform at BMO Following Q4 Results; Price Target Raised to C$14.50

BMO Capital Markets on Thursday maintained its outperform rating on the shares of Orla Mining while raising it price target to C$14.50 from C$12.50 following the company's fourth-quarter results.

data.title: Trump to Sign Order Aimed at 'Eliminating' Education Department — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton

President Donald Trump will sign an executive order Thursday aimed at dismantling the Education Department, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed.

"The President is finally taking much-needed action to return education to where it belongs, and that's to educators closest to students in their classrooms in their respective states," Leavitt told reporters Thursday morning.

Trump has long said that the department's duties should be turned over to the states. While closing the 4,000-person department requires an act of Congress, the administration has already made moves to shrink it, including announcing last week that it was cutting its staff by nearly 50%. The department will "continue to deliver on all statutory programs that fall under the agency's purview, including formula funding, student loans, Pell Grants, funding for special needs students, and competitive grantmaking," Education Secretary Linda McMahon said in announcing the cuts. In an interview with SiriusXM earlier this week, McMahon said the department's role is "to help provide funding so that the states themselves can help with their own programs," adding that the creativity and innovation has to come from the states themselves.

A group of 21 attorneys general sued last week to block the department's planned job cuts, arguing that the move is "equivalent to incapacitating key, statutorily-mandated functions of the Department."

A federal judge in Maryland district court ordered the department to reinstate probationary workers because their terminations weren't for performance, but instead were illegally conducted reductions in force. The administration is appealing the ruling.

The department oversees some $1.6 trillion in federally-guaranteed student loans held by millions of Americans. Scaling back its operations could help the for-profit education industry if it leads to less federal oversight.

Shares of the for-profit firm Adtalem Global Education, which owns former DeVry schools, were up slightly Thursday morning and have doubled over the past year. The stock price of Grand Canyon Education, another for-profit operator, was basically flat, but is up 28% over the last 12 months.

Write to Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: First Helium Trading Lower as It Sees Oil and Gas Output from Two Alberta Wells
data.description:
First Helium on Thursday said it made two discoveries at its Worsley Project in Alberta.

The company said its 7-30 well is produced 50 barrels of heavy oil over the two days following an stimulation. It also tested a second well, which flowed natural gas with helium content of 1.0%.

"Our recent drilling program has validated what we've long understood about the multi-zone potential of our Worsley property," said chief executive Ed Bereznicki. "While our primary oil target in the Leduc formation did not test as commercially viable, our secondary target for heavy oil has exceeded our expectations for inflow of cold flow heavy oil from a vertical well bore."

The company's shares were last seen down $0.01 to $0.035 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: Nigerian pipeline carrying Bonny resumes pumping
data.description:
Crude oil flowing into Nigeria's Trans Niger Pipeline has been routed through an alternative line after blasts ruptured the main line on Tuesday, the pipeline's operator said, pointing to a quick resolution to the outage.

* The update on the re-routing was given by a spokesperson for operator Renaissance late on Wednesday.

* Force majeure was not declared on loadings of Bonny Light crude and loadings of the grade have continued from storage, traders said.

* Differentials of competing grades did not react to the outage, a trader said, adding that the swift resolution could put downward pressure on Bonny Light's value.

* Bonny Light for April 23-24 was offered at dated Brent plus $2.00 earlier this week, down 50 cents from offers last week.

* On Angolan grades, April-loading cargoes are most if not all sold, a trader said, and there were some early offers of May cargoes.

* Dalia for May loading was offered at dated Brent plus $1.80, a source said.
